TR_Dynamics+1+UnknownJournal.prediction: Thermal part supported; electrochemical part not. Therefore, positive tab electrochemical stress is greatest at 3C, not 4C. The mechanistic implication is that thermal and electrochemical tab stresses are decoupled and peak at different C-rates. A tab-level risk assessment must evaluate both dimensions independently; using 4C as the single worst-case condition would underestimate electrochemical stress at the positive tab during 3C operation.

TR_Dynamics+10+Batteries.prediction: SEI film formation is amenable because its degradation mechanism and governing equations are already established, so small-perturbation/parameter identification can be applied to fit unknown parameters within a known model structure. The pore-blocking phenomenon is not amenable because its governing equations/model structure are not yet known; the limitation is that perturbative parameter identification cannot be used to identify parameters for an unestablished (nonexistent) governing model.

For the newly observed pore-blocking phenomenon, the approach cannot be applied because the appropriate governing equations are non-existent or not well established. Without these equations, there is no mathematical basis for formulating the perturbative terms required by the method. The limitation is therefore not computational but structural: the method presupposes a known mechanistic framework, which is absent for poorly understood degradation modes.
TR_Dynamics+10+Batteries.reason: Prediction matches the gold: SEI is amenable because established governing equations exist enabling perturbative terms; pore-blocking is not because governing equations/model structure are not established, a structural (not computational) limitation.

TR_Dynamics+11+UnknownJournal.prediction: (1) With large-radius cylindrical cells, axial (along-the-axis) airflow has limited ability to remove internally generated heat because the dominant heat path is radial; the larger the radius, the longer the conduction distance to the surface and the larger the core-to-surface temperature rise, so axial airflow cannot effectively mitigate core hot spots. (2) A thin cell stack is preferred for the solid polymer electrolyte module at high discharge rates because reduced thickness shortens ion/heat transport paths, lowering internal resistance and polarization while improving heat removal, thereby reducing temperature gradients and enabling higher-rate operation.
TR_Dynamics+11+UnknownJournal.gold_answer: For the axial airflow strategy, directing airflow parallel to the cylinder axis is effective at reducing surface temperature. However, when the battery has a large radius, the hottest internal temperature remains high despite effective surface cooling, because the airflow cannot adequately remove heat from the cell core. This means surface temperature reduction does not guarantee safe internal temperatures for large-radius cells. For the solid polymer electrolyte module under high discharge rates, cell stacks with small thickness are preferred. Thin stacks minimize temperature gradients across the stack and prevent the battery temperature from rising excessively under high discharge conditions. Thicker stacks would allow larger thermal gradients to develop, increasing the risk of localized overheating.

TR_Dynamics+12+Batteries.gold_answer: (1) At 90–100% SOC, released flammable gases accumulated in the reactor vessel and exceeded the lower flammability limit, triggering a gas explosion that added combustion-driven pressure on top of the gas-release contribution, producing pressures above 7 bar that gas release alone cannot account for. TR_Dynamics+13+Batteries.prediction:

TR_Dynamics+13+Batteries.gold_answer: Voltage drop as early warning: The evidence supports that voltage drop precedes the sudden temperature rise marking TR onset by 2–8 seconds across tested batteries, making it a valid early warning indicator within this experimental context. The absence of HF is a condition-specific result, not evidence of inherent HF-free behavior across all environments.

TR_Dynamics+15+Batteries.prediction: The released gas presents a credible explosion risk because the combined flammable mixture can fall within its calculated flammability window (LEL 7 vol% to UEL 31 vol%), and key contributors reach substantial concentrations (CO up to 16.85 vol%, CH4 up to 7.6 vol%, C2H4 up to 7.86 vol%), so the mixture can be ignitable even if some components (HCN and C2H6) are below their individual limits. One limitation is that the assessment uses calculated mixture limits (Le Chatelier) and measured surface temperature, but does not account for real-world dilution/mixing, confinement/ventilation, or ignition source availability in the actual environment.

Together, fuel concentration and ignition energy conditions are simultaneously satisfied. TR_Dynamics+16+Batteries.prediction:
At least two hazard dimensions where LFP is not safer than NCM (based on sealed-chamber evidence and general engineering reasoning) are:
1) Flammable gas (fuel) release / explosion propensity: Lower peak pressure/temperature/TRD does not imply less fuel. LFP cells can still vent substantial quantities of combustible electrolyte/solvent vapor; their lower heat release may simply reduce secondary burning intensity while the amount and ignitability of released gases (and thus deflagration hazard if an ignition source is present) may be comparable.
2) Toxic/corrosive inhalation hazard from vent products: “Safer” cannot be concluded from pressure/temperature/TRD alone because those metrics do not measure composition. LFP vent streams can still contain hazardous species (e.g., HF and other irritant/toxic electrolyte decomposition products). Lower thermal severity may not reduce, and can sometimes shift, the product distribution toward volatile toxic compounds; thus the inhalation/corrosive exposure hazard may not be lower than for NCM.
TR_Dynamics+16+Batteries.gold_answer: First, post-TR vent gas explosion risk is higher for LFP than NCM. LFP vent gas contains ~20% H₂ versus ~10% in NCM. Because H₂ has a significantly lower explosion threshold than CO, the higher H₂ fraction makes LFP vent gas more prone to explosion despite LFP's lower peak temperature and pressure. Second, early warning lead time is shorter for LFP cells. Gas release precedes fire ejection by only ~987 s in LFP versus ~1585 s in NCM, and gas release precedes voltage drop by only ~391 s in LFP versus ~1119 s in NCM. This shorter warning window reduces available response time, representing a distinct safety disadvantage for LFP in detection-dependent safety systems.
TR_Dynamics+16+Batteries.reason: Gives a partly aligned dimension (explosion/deflagration risk from vent gas), but does not include the required mechanism that LFP has higher H2 fraction (~20% vs ~10%) and that H2’s lower explosion threshold drives higher explosion risk. The second dimension (toxic/corrosive products) is speculative and not supported by the described experiments, and it misses the required shorter early-warning lead time for LFP (~987 s vs ~1585 s).

TR_Dynamics+17+Batteries.prediction: With λ=1, FFRLS reduces mechanically to standard RLS: past data are never down-weighted, so the estimator behaves as if parameters are constant and old samples dominate the normal equations. The concern is the classic RLS failure mode of covariance/parameter “freezing” (loss of tracking and poor adaptation to time-varying battery parameters, often accompanied by ill-conditioning/windup when the excitation changes), because the gain shrinks as P collapses and new information has little effect. Mitigation is to use λ slightly below 1 (0<λ<1), i.e., decrease λ from 1 to a value close to 1 (typically around 0.95–0.999 depending on noise vs. tracking), which increases forgetting and restores tracking. Beyond λ tuning, a second-order RC model adds an identifiability/conditioning challenge: it contains two dynamic time constants that can be close together or weakly excited in typical drive data, making the regressor columns highly correlated and the parameter set not persistently excited. This causes ambiguous separation of the two RC branches and poor observability of individual parameters even if the algorithm is made more adaptive by reducing λ.
TR_Dynamics+17+Batteries.gold_answer: Setting λ=1 in FFRLS is equivalent to no forgetting, meaning old data accumulates without discounting. As the number of identification cycles increases, accumulated old data increasingly dominates the covariance matrix update, eventually suppressing the algorithm's sensitivity to new parameter changes — this is the data saturation failure mode identical to standard RLS. To mitigate this, λ should be set below 1 (within the typical range of 0.95 to 1), where smaller λ increases forgetting speed, allowing recent data to carry more weight. However, λ tuning alone cannot resolve the structural challenge of the second-order RC model: it requires identifying five parameters from only two measured signals (voltage and current). Furthermore, the two RC time constants correspond to separate response links, and identifying one link may cause information loss or data saturation in the other, a problem that persists regardless of forgetting factor selection.
TR_Dynamics+17+Batteries.reason: Prediction correctly states λ=1 means no forgetting/equivalent to standard RLS, explains old-data domination leading to gain shrinkage/freezing (data saturation/loss of tracking), and recommends decreasing λ slightly below 1 with a typical ~0.95–0.999 range. However, it misses the key gold-point that the second-order RC model involves identifying five parameters from only two measured signals, and it does not clearly mention the specific ‘two identification links where identifying one causes information loss/data saturation in the other’ mechanism (it instead frames it as correlated time constants/persistent excitation).

TR_Dynamics+18+Batteries.prediction:
The voltage drop and the first safety venting are triggered by internal cell conditions (electrochemical breakdown/SEI collapse and separator/electrode reactions for the voltage drop, then rapid gas generation raising internal pressure to the vent opening threshold for venting). These thresholds depend mainly on the cell’s internal materials and SOC and only weakly on the external ambient pressure/temperature, so the temperatures at which they occur stay essentially fixed.

After venting, the process becomes dominated by heat release/heat removal and by how the vented gases and any leaked electrolyte behave outside the cell. Lower ambient temperature increases the heat sink and lowers the cell temperature rise, and lower ambient pressure changes vent flow, gas density/oxygen availability and combustion intensity, reducing external burning/heat feedback to the cell. This slows the self-heating escalation, so a higher temperature is required to reach thermal runaway onset, and once runaway occurs the peak surface temperature, temperature rise rate, and mass loss are reduced.

The phase most affected by ambient pressure is the post-vent stage (vented-gas/electrolyte discharge and any external combustion/heat feedback), because ambient pressure directly controls the expansion/mixing/combustion of the released products and therefore the net heat fed back to the cell and the amount of material expelled/consumed.
TR_Dynamics+18+Batteries.gold_answer: The voltage drop and safety venting are governed by internal electrochemical and mechanical thresholds (separator integrity, valve mechanical pressure limit) that are intrinsic to the cell and independent of external ambient conditions, explaining their insensitivity. In contrast, post-vent dynamics are sensitive because: (1) after the safety valve opens, ambient oxygen concentration (which decreases at 40 kPa) directly governs combustion intensity and mass loss; (2) lower ambient temperature increases heat dissipation and lowers the initial thermal state, suppressing internal side reaction intensity; (3) these combined effects raise the energy required to reach runaway onset, elevating the onset temperature. The evidence explicitly states that ambient pressure impact is manifested primarily in the period from safety valve opening to thermal runaway onset, not during initial heating, consistent with the oxygen-driven combustion mechanism becoming active only after venting.
TR_Dynamics+18+Batteries.reason: Prediction matches the gold mechanism: early indicators (voltage drop, venting) set by intrinsic internal electrochemical/mechanical thresholds, hence insensitive to ambient conditions; post-vent behavior depends on heat balance and oxygen-limited combustion, so reduced pressure/temperature lowers combustion/heat feedback and increases heat loss, raising TR onset temperature and reducing peak T/rate/mass loss. It correctly identifies the pressure-sensitive phase as post-vent (between vent opening and TR onset). Minor gap: it mentions oxygen availability generally but not as explicitly tied to reduced oxygen concentration at 40 kPa, and it slightly broadens to vent-flow effects.

TR_Dynamics+20+Batteries.prediction:
Nanosizing can improve rate capability by shortening solid-state diffusion length and increasing surface area for charge-transfer, but it also raises specific surface area–driven side reactions (more SEI growth on anodes, more CEI/electrolyte oxidation on high-voltage cathodes), increasing irreversible capacity loss, gas generation, impedance rise, and heat release. Higher surface area can accelerate parasitic reactions especially at elevated temperature/overcharge, lowering calendar life and potentially worsening safety (more exothermic interphase formation/decomposition, more reactive surface for thermal runaway onset). Nanoscale powders also pack less efficiently (lower tap density), increasing inactive surface per stored Ah and reducing volumetric energy density; they can create higher moisture/air reactivity during processing and dust/explosion handling concerns. Mechanically, small particles can reduce intraparticle cracking (helping life), but high surface reactivity often dominates unless coatings/electrolyte additives are optimized.

Using a broad particle size distribution can increase electrode packing density (small particles fill voids between large ones), improving volumetric energy density and potentially lowering overall surface area per volume (reducing interfacial side reactions and heat generation), which can benefit safety and calendar life. However, wide PSD introduces performance nonuniformity: large particles become diffusion-limited at high C-rate (lower power), while small particles may carry disproportionate current (higher local reaction rate), leading to localized overpotential, lithium plating risk on the anode at fast charge, and heterogeneous SEI growth. Broad PSD can also complicate slurry rheology and electrode drying, increasing risk of binder/carbon segregation and tortuosity gradients that worsen local current hotspots. Net: nanosizing trades higher power for lower volumetric energy density and potentially higher reactivity/safety risk; broad PSD trades higher packing/energy density and possibly lower average reactivity for reduced high-rate performance and increased heterogeneity-driven safety/performance issues if not carefully engineered.
TR_Dynamics+20+Batteries.gold_answer: Reducing particle size to nanoscale shortens Li+ ion transport length and lowers diffusion barriers, improving rate performance. However, smaller particles increase surface area, promoting more side reactions that can cause thermal issues and internal short circuits. The claim that smaller particles 'always improve safety' is incorrect. A broad particle size distribution increases energy density but introduces poor cell homogeneity due to particle size variance and surface energy differences, creating uneven stress during cycling. A uniform size distribution, while harder to produce, reduces stress strains during charging and improves cycle performance. Neither strategy is universally superior; each involves a performance-safety trade-off that must be matched to application requirements.
TR_Dynamics+20+Batteries.reason: Prediction correctly explains nanosizing improves rate via shorter diffusion length but increases surface-area-driven side reactions and can worsen safety (heat/thermal runaway onset), explicitly rejecting the idea that smaller is always safer. It also captures broad PSD improving packing/energy density but causing heterogeneity/nonuniform reaction and associated risks. However, it misses the gold’s specific point that a uniform size distribution reduces stress strains and improves cycle performance, and it does not mention internal short circuits explicitly (though thermal/safety risks are covered).

TR_Dynamics+23+Batteries.prediction: A lower maximum vent-gas temperature means the cell reached less severe thermal runaway, so less of the electrolyte solvent (DMC) was thermally decomposed/oxidized or consumed in reactions inside the cell. As a result, more intact (residual) DMC survives to evaporate and be expelled in the vent gas, giving a higher measured mmol/Ah. This implies the cathode material strongly governs high-temperature reaction intensity: NVPF’s cathode behavior at elevated temperatures is less heat/oxygen-releasing and less reactive with electrolyte than the others, limiting peak temperature and therefore limiting solvent destruction.
TR_Dynamics+23+Batteries.gold_answer: At elevated temperatures, cathode degradation accelerates and releases more oxygen. This oxygen facilitates combustion of electrolyte solvent within the cell, converting a greater proportion of solvent into gaseous combustion products (CO, CO2) rather than leaving it as unburned solvent vapor. NVPF cells reach only 265°C, so cathode degradation and oxygen release are limited, leaving more electrolyte solvent uncombusted and detectable in vent gas. Conversely, NMC cells reach 1050°C, driving extensive cathode oxygen release and near-complete solvent combustion, yielding minimal residual DMC. The implication is that cathode thermal stability governs the extent of in-situ solvent combustion: chemistries with lower peak temperatures preserve more solvent in vent gas.
TR_Dynamics+23+Batteries.reason: Prediction captures the key link that lower peak temperature means less solvent is consumed/oxidized, leaving more residual DMC in vent gas, and it attributes this to cathode governing reaction intensity/oxygen release. However, it is less explicit about the specific mechanism of cathode degradation releasing oxygen that drives in-situ solvent combustion to CO/CO2, and instead frames it more generally as less severe runaway/less decomposition.

TR_Dynamics+24+Nano_Energy.prediction:
The claim is incorrect: non-flammability of an SSE does not eliminate thermal runaway because electrochemical instability can still generate heat.

Two relevant SSE failure modes in a high-voltage NMC / possible Li-metal configuration are:
1) Oxidative decomposition at the cathode (high-voltage instability): If the SSE is not stable at the NMC operating potential, it can oxidize and form resistive interphases and/or release reaction heat. The growing impedance drives higher overpotential and localized Joule heating during charge/discharge, which can accelerate further decomposition (autocatalytic heating) and trigger thermal runaway even without a flammable liquid.
2) Reductive decomposition and dendrite-enabled internal short at the anode (Li-metal instability): Under overcharge, Li plating/metal formation can reduce the SSE or penetrate it as dendrites through defects/grain boundaries. This creates an electronically conductive path or a hard short. The short causes rapid current flow and intense ohmic heating; concurrent SSE reduction and interphase growth also produce heat, together potentially leading to runaway.
TR_Dynamics+24+Nano_Energy.gold_answer: The claim is not supported. SSEs face two electrochemical stability failure modes in this configuration. First, lithium metal generated under overcharging can reduce the SSE, producing heat from the reductive decomposition reaction at the anode/SSE interface. Second, the high-voltage NMC cathode can oxidize the SSE at the cathode/SSE interface, also generating heat. Evidence states that nearly no SSE can be simultaneously stable against both lithium metal and high-voltage cathodes. Both reductive and oxidative reactions produce heat, enabling continuous heat accumulation that may lead to thermal runaway. Non-flammability of the SSE does not preclude exothermic interfacial reactions, so the engineer's claim conflates flammability with electrochemical stability.
TR_Dynamics+24+Nano_Energy.reason: Prediction correctly rejects the claim and identifies the two key electrochemical failure modes: SSE oxidation at the high-voltage NMC cathode and SSE reduction by Li metal at the anode, both contributing to heat generation and potential runaway; it also notes non-flammability doesn’t remove TR risk. However, it adds an internal-short/dendrite mechanism and Joule-heating emphasis not required by the gold, and it omits the explicit point that nearly no SSE is simultaneously stable against both Li metal and high-voltage cathodes.

TR_Dynamics+25+Journal_of_The_Electroche.prediction: Reducing electrode porosity changes heat generation mainly by increasing ionic transport resistance in the electrolyte-filled pore network: lower porosity reduces effective electrolyte conductivity and raises concentration/ohmic polarization, so I^2R and entropy-related heating associated with overpotential increase, tending to increase cell temperature at 1C (despite slightly less liquid volume/heat capacity). Reducing electrode thickness changes heat generation mainly by shortening transport path lengths for both ions and electrons and reducing total internal resistance and polarization, so irreversible (ohmic/charge-transfer) heat decreases and temperature tends to decrease (also with less active material/heat capacity, but the dominant effect at fixed 1C is lower heat generation). Therefore the two modifications have opposite directional effects on cell temperature: lower porosity increases temperature, thinner electrodes decrease temperature.
TR_Dynamics+25+Journal_of_The_Electroche.gold_answer: Reducing electrode porosity increases cell temperature because more active material is packed into the same volume, increasing the total heat-generating capacity of the cell. Reducing electrode thickness decreases cell temperature because thinner electrodes operate at lower concentration overpotentials, reducing the heating rate per unit time. The two modifications produce opposite directional effects: lower porosity raises temperature via increased active material content, while thinner electrodes lower temperature via reduced concentration overpotential losses. These are distinct mechanisms—one acts through total energy content and the other through the rate of irreversible heat generation from transport limitations.
TR_Dynamics+25+Journal_of_The_Electroche.reason: Correct on direction (porosity reduction raises temperature; thickness reduction lowers temperature) and identifies distinct mechanisms. However, the porosity mechanism is mismatched: prediction attributes higher temperature mainly to increased ionic transport resistance/polarization, while the gold requires increased active material content/total heat-generating capacity and explicitly discourages attributing it to ionic conductivity changes. Thickness mechanism is broadly aligned (reduced transport losses/overpotential), though less specifically tied to concentration overpotential.

TR_Dynamics+27+RSC_Advances.prediction: (1) In the oven, heating is relatively uniform and the cell is surrounded by hot air; when the safety valve opens, high-pressure gases/electrolyte vapor vent and expand, carrying away enthalpy and producing strong convective/evaporative cooling at the vent and inside the cell, so the measured cell temperature can drop temporarily before exothermic reactions accelerate to thermal runaway. (3) With plate heating the environment above the cell is cooler and oxygen mixing is limited; vented products are diluted/condense and may be nonflammable/oxygen-starved, and jet cooling during venting reduces flame stabilization, so although thermal runaway occurs inside the cell, external ignition/combustion is not sustained.
TR_Dynamics+27+RSC_Advances.gold_answer: (1) When the safety valve opens at ~168 °C under oven conditions, high-temperature gases are vented from the cell interior. These gases carry heat away from the cell, causing a temporary net heat loss that produces the observed temperature dip before exothermic reactions resume and drive thermal runaway. (2) Under the heating plate condition, tight contact with the cell surface creates more intense and localized heating, accelerating side reactions. Faster side reactions generate gas more rapidly, so internal pressure reaches the valve threshold almost simultaneously with the onset of uncontrolled exothermic reactions, eliminating the temporal separation seen in the oven case. (3) Although thermal runaway releases combustible gases and particles, the compact stacking arrangement under the heating plate constrains heat dissipation to the surrounding environment. The ambient temperature therefore remains below the ignition threshold of the released combustibles, preventing ignition and combustion.

TR_Dynamics+30+Electronics.prediction: The claim is incomplete/misleading in at least these ways: (1) “Produces less gas” does not automatically mean “safer,” because the vented gas composition and flammability window matter. An LFP cell can vent a mixture with a higher fraction of flammable species (e.g., H2/CO/hydrocarbons), and if that mixture falls within flammability limits in air it can ignite readily; conversely an NCM cell may generate more total gas but with more inert/CO2 content that can dilute below the lower flammability limit. Mechanism: different cathode chemistries and electrolyte decomposition pathways change the H2/CO/CO2 balance, shifting whether the vent plume is fuel-rich, oxygen-rich, or diluted. (2) “Higher trigger temperature” does not guarantee benign outcomes because vent-valve behavior and pressure rise can dominate the hazard. If LFP generates gas faster relative to its free volume or vents later/sticks, it can reach higher internal pressure before opening, producing a more forceful jet and better fuel–air mixing, increasing ignition probability even if onset is at higher temperature. Mechanism: gas–solid ratios and rate of gas generation vs. vent capacity control pressure dynamics; delayed/abrupt venting can create an explosive aerosol/jet. (3) Comparing chemistries without normalizing to gas–solid ratio (gas generated per mass of active materials) can invert conclusions: an LFP pack may vent a smaller absolute volume per cell but, per unit energy or per unit mass, the gas generation and resulting concentration in an enclosure can still reach flammable limits as readily as NCM. Mechanism: energy density differences and mass/volume scaling change enclosure concentration and whether the mixture enters the flammable range.
TR_Dynamics+30+Electronics.gold_answer: First, while LFP produces less total gas per Ah (0.569 L/Ah vs 1.814–2.752 L/Ah for NCM), LFP TR gas contains ~50.82% H2, which has a low lower flammability limit. This makes LFP gas more flammable per unit volume than NCM gas, reversing the hazard ranking on a flammability basis: LFP > NCM811 > NCM622 > NCM523 > NCM905. Second, LFP's vent valve opens before TR due to pressure buildup, not TR onset, meaning early venting releases flammable H2-rich gas into the environment without the TR warning signal that NCM cells provide. The higher trigger temperature reduces ignition probability at the cell level but does not reduce the flammability hazard of already-vented gas. Additionally, LFP's gas-solid ratio (~50.6) far exceeds NCM's (0.5–1.6), indicating that a much larger fraction of LFP mass loss is gaseous, amplifying dispersion risk.
TR_Dynamics+30+Electronics.reason: Prediction correctly challenges the blanket claim by noting that lower gas volume doesn’t imply lower hazard because composition/LFL matters, and it states higher trigger temperature doesn’t eliminate risk from vented flammable gas. It also mentions gas–solid ratio as an important normalization factor. However, it misses key required specifics: the strong H2-rich nature of LFP gas and explicit inversion/ranking of flammability hazard vs gas-volume ranking, and it gets vent-valve behavior wrong relative to the evidence (gold: LFP vents before TR; prediction speculates about later/sticking/delayed venting).

TR_Dynamics+31+Energies.prediction:
At room temperature and 10 It, the initial voltage collapse is dominated by kinetic/ohmic polarization (charge-transfer and transport limits) rather than true depletion of active lithium. If discharge is continued, two sequential relaxations occur: (1) heat generation and time under load reduce resistance and overpotential (higher ionic conductivity and faster reaction kinetics), lifting cell voltage; then (2) concentration gradients in electrolyte/porous electrodes relax and lithium redistributes/diffuses to previously underutilized active material, so more of the stored lithium becomes accessible and the delivered capacity approaches low-rate capacity.

At −18 °C, the same “self-warming + diffusion/redistribution” rescue is ineffective because temperature-driven kinetic and transport limitations become dominant and persist: ionic conductivity and solid-state diffusion are much lower, charge-transfer is sluggish, and polarization can remain so large that voltage limits are reached before meaningful redistribution/use of active material occurs. The result is strongly non-linear, history-dependent behavior (high Peukert number and unpredictable energy density) that cannot be corrected by the room-temperature recovery pathway.
TR_Dynamics+31+Energies.gold_answer: At 10 It and room temperature, the large discharge current generates substantial Joule heating, raising the cell temperature significantly (to approximately 43 °C). This temperature increase reduces internal resistance, allowing the cell voltage to recover above the 2 V cutoff and enabling the discharge to continue to near-full capacity. The recovery depends on Joule heating being sufficient to overcome the initial resistance-driven voltage drop. At −18 °C, internal resistance is dramatically elevated, causing a large voltage drop and severely reduced discharge capacity reflected in the Peukert number of 1.85. The energy density behavior at −18 °C is non-linear and cannot be predicted by Peukert relationships. Joule heating at this temperature is insufficient to overcome the extreme resistance increase, so the self-heating recovery mechanism that operates at room temperature does not restore capacity under deep sub-zero conditions.
TR_Dynamics+31+Energies.reason: Matches the core mechanism: high-rate discharge causes self-heating/Joule heating that raises temperature, reduces resistance/overpotential, and lets voltage recover above cutoff to access near-full capacity; and at −18 °C the very high resistance/kinetic limits make self-heating insufficient, yielding non-linear behavior not well captured by Peukert. However, it omits the specific Peukert number (1.85) and adds an extra diffusion/redistribution step not present in the gold.

TR_Dynamics+32+Energies.prediction:

Restricting SOC to 15–90%: it avoids the steep end regions where LFP OCV changes rapidly and exhibits stronger hysteresis/relaxation and where SOC errors and voltage measurement errors translate into large SOC errors; it also avoids operating near saturation/depletion where dynamics are highly nonlinear.

Remaining accuracy risk: systematic SOC bias in the mid-SOC range even with fixed m,n and restricted SOC.

Root cause: temperature/aging-dependent shifts of the OCV curve (especially the plateau level/offset and low-frequency hysteresis/relaxation effects) that are not fully captured by the fixed-shape model; because LFP has a very flat OCV–SOC plateau, small unmodeled OCV offsets translate into large SOC errors.

This reduces computational burden without sacrificing accuracy. Restricting operation to 15%–90% SOC is justified because the SOC-OCV model estimation errors for LFP stay within 0.5% in this range but grow larger outside it, making the excluded regions unreliable for estimation. The remaining accuracy risk is larger SOC estimation errors at partial SOC when using a first-order RC model with constant resistance and capacitance, because actual R and C values at partial SOC differ considerably from those at low and high SOC, violating the constant-parameter assumption.

The remaining-risk explanation diverges from the gold: it cites OCV offset/hysteresis on the plateau rather than the first-order RC model constant R,C causing larger errors at partial SOC due to R,C variation.

TR_Dynamics+34+Energies.prediction:
Limitation 1 (EIS‑identified equivalent‑circuit parameters): Parameters obtained from EIS are typically small‑signal and frequency‑domain estimates around a given operating point (SOC, temperature, bias current). When the battery is driven by varying/large load currents, the cell behaves nonlinearly (current‑dependent polarization/charge‑transfer behavior and state dependence), so the “fixed” EIS RC/R values no longer represent the time‑domain response under those conditions, leading to model mismatch.

Limitation 2 (adding RC branches to a Thevenin model): Increasing the number of RC branches improves fit but quickly runs into identifiability/over‑parameterization limits—multiple RC networks can produce nearly the same terminal‑voltage dynamics over the available data bandwidth, especially with noise and limited excitation. This makes parameters poorly conditioned and unstable to estimate (and prone to overfitting), so added branches do not reliably translate into better predictive accuracy.
TR_Dynamics+34+Energies.gold_answer: First limitation: EIS-based parameter identification cannot capture parameter dependence on current. Because EIS is a small-signal, linear technique applied at a fixed operating point, the current-dependent nonlinearities in battery parameters cannot be extracted from EIS measurements. This means the identified parameters will be inaccurate when load current deviates significantly from the identification condition, undermining the model's validity across varying load profiles. Second limitation: While adding more RC branches to a Thevenin model improves the accuracy of reproduced dynamic behavior, this benefit is bounded by the accuracy of the parameter identification method. If the parameters themselves are current-independent (as EIS yields), adding RC branches refines the dynamic shape but does not correct the fundamental current-dependency gap, and increasing model complexity adds computational cost without resolving the EIS limitation.

The prediction instead focuses on identifiability/overparameterization/overfitting, which is plausible but not the stated limitation mechanism in the gold.

This means that thermal isolation or increased thermal resistance at the shell interface—rather than at the pole connector—is the higher-leverage design intervention for slowing or preventing propagation. TR_Dynamics+40+ENERGY_andamp;_ENVIRONMEN.prediction: Fluorinated electrolyte suppresses the direct cathode–electrolyte reaction (so the cathode mixed with electrolyte shows only mild exotherm), but it does not remove the cathode as the main oxidizing heat source once a full cell runs away. This Ca+An cross‑talk uses the cathode (especially delithiated NMC) as an oxidant for anode-derived fuels, releasing large heat even though cathode–electrolyte reactivity is low. Once full-cell thermal runaway is triggered—primarily by anode-electrolyte reactions (66.7% of heat at T2)—the cathode undergoes oxygen release at high temperature. This released oxygen then reacts directly with the lithiated anode (Ca+An cross-talk), a reaction independent of electrolyte oxidation resistance. The Ca+An reaction contributes 66% of total post-runaway heat. Therefore, fluorinated electrolyte removes one cathode heat pathway but cannot block the thermally-driven cathode oxygen release and its subsequent reaction with the anode, which dominates total energy release.

TR_Dynamics+5+Electrochemistry.prediction: As pack size increases, earlier failing cells vent into an increasingly gas-rich, oxygen-lean sealed volume, so later cells’ releases burn less completely (more inert dilution and reduced oxidizer availability), reducing the fraction of chemical energy converted to pressure. In addition, the larger pack has greater thermal/structural inertia and more internal surfaces/obstacles, which absorb heat and dissipate/attenuate shock and slow deflagration, lowering peak overpressure from each subsequent event. With more cells, these effects compound because each rupture adds more inert/combustion products and turbulence/flow resistance and preloads the enclosure, progressively suppressing combustion efficiency and pressure rise per additional battery, hence lower explosion pressure and TNT equivalent per battery.
TR_Dynamics+5+Electrochemistry.gold_answer: As pack size increases, two compounding mechanisms reduce per-battery explosion severity. First, heat conduction distance from the initially triggered battery to subsequent batteries grows, lowering the heat conduction rate and reducing the thermal energy acquired by each unexploded battery per unit time. This weakens the driving force for successive thermal runaway events. Second, oxygen inside the sealed barrel is progressively consumed by earlier explosions and combustion of ejected flammable gases and electrolyte. Reduced oxygen availability causes inadequate combustion of subsequent ejected material, lowering the chemical energy released per event. Together, these effects mean each successive battery explodes under both lower thermal input and lower oxidant supply, producing lower blast pressure and lower TNT equivalent per battery. The effect compounds because each additional battery further depletes oxygen and extends conduction distance.

Thus partially correct.

T2 remains nearly constant because it is governed by separator melting temperature, which is a material property unaffected by internal gas generation from overcharge. The safety implication is critical: at 41 °C, T1 falls within the normal operating temperature range of a battery thermal management system. This means that even during routine operation at 100% SOC after a single overcharge event, the cell may spontaneously enter self-heating and trigger thermal runaway without any additional abuse, despite appearing electrochemically normal based on capacity alone.
